steps.title: Welcome to OpenCatalogi
steps.body: A couple of quick steps to get you publishing. OpenCatalogi turns your catalogs into open, discoverable publications — let's create your first catalog and connect you to the federated network.

steps.title: Publishing prerequisites
steps.body: OpenCatalogi publishes from OpenRegister. The catalog, publication and listing registers are configured automatically on install — this just verifies them. If OpenRegister was enabled after OpenCatalogi, running this re-imports the configuration.

steps.title: Default catalog scope
steps.body: Publishing in OpenCatalogi is governed by schema-RBAC (not a per-object published flag), and every catalog needs a scope. Pick the default visibility for the catalog you're about to create — you can still tighten or widen it later.

steps.title: Create your first catalog
steps.body: A catalog is the container your publications live in. We'll create your first one using the scope you just chose — you can rename it and add publications afterwards.

steps.title: Connect to the federated network
steps.body: OpenCatalogi is a federated network: instances share their published catalogs so they can be discovered together. Connect to the national directory now to see federated catalogs in your Directory — or skip this and it will sync automatically later.

steps.title: All set
steps.body: OpenCatalogi is ready. Open Catalogs to manage what you publish, or the Directory to discover federated catalogs.

steps.title: Welcome to OpenCatalogi
steps.body: Let's walk the publishing journey together — from an empty install to a catalog the world can discover. You'll create each record yourself, in your own workspace.
Everything you publish lives in a catalog. Open Catalogs from the menu.
Create your first catalog — it's the container your publications will live in.
Open the catalog you just created to manage what it publishes — its publications open inside it.
steps.task: Open your catalog and view its Publications

steps.body: A publication is a single thing you make open — a document, dataset or record. Add your first one.

steps.title: Publishing is access-controlled
steps.body: OpenCatalogi has no separate 'publish' switch — visibility is governed by schema-RBAC and the catalog's scope. Grant the public (or a group) read access on the publication schema, within the catalog scope you chose, and the publication becomes discoverable.
Published catalogs are found through the Directory — your own and federated ones. Open it to see the discovery side.

steps.title: You're publishing
steps.body: That's the full loop: catalog → publication → schema-RBAC publishing → discovery. Externally, your published catalogs are harvested as DCAT-AP, so registers and portals can find and re-list your publications automatically.

observability._note: ADR-040 declarative observability. OpenCatalogi adopts OpenRegister's AppHost engine: the /api/health and /api/metrics routes are aliased to AppHost\Controller\GenericHealth#index / GenericMetrics#index in appinfo/routes.php, and the engine reads this block at request time. The hand-written HealthController + MetricsController were deleted. Parity vs the pre-adoption contract: the two health checks (database critical, filesystem degraded) and search_requests_total map 1:1 to declarative descriptors; the JSON-field-grouped domain metrics (publications/catalogs/listings by status+catalog, directory entries, and the usageCounter view/download sums by catalog) are reproduced byte-for-byte by OpenCatalogiMetricsProvider via the {kind:provider} escape hatch, which runs the exact same OpenRegister-backed queries the deleted controller ran. Documented intentional improvements (matching OpenRegister's own adoption): the health JSON now carries an `app` field; the implicit opencatalogi_up gauge is engine-owned (always 1 once the request is served) rather than mirroring the DB check.

metrics.name: domain
metrics.help: OpenCatalogi domain metrics (publications, catalogs, listings, directory entries, usage view/download totals) reproduced via the provider escape hatch with the same OpenRegister-backed queries as the pre-adoption MetricsController.

pages.title: WOO batches
pages._note: WOO disclosure-batch list via the generic index renderer over the WOO register/schema (ADR-022 — reuses the lib, no bespoke table). Per-batch detail (deck board widget + WOO tabs) is the WooBatchDetail custom page.

pages.title: WOO batch
pages._note: Surfaces the OpenRegister deck-board widget (queue/board — not bespoke) plus WOO-specific surfaces: progress summary, redaction review, inventarislijst download, ready-for-review/publish (approval-workflow gated).

pages.title: Dashboard
pages._note: Custom analytics dashboard (charts + recent activity + per-catalog stats). Could be type:"dashboard" with widget composition once chart widgets land.

pages.title: Catalog
pages._note: CnDetailPage with bespoke header (catalog stats + slug + edit actions) + sidebar tabs. Lib gap: header-component slot resolution from manifest, granular sidebar-tab declaration.

pages.title: Publication
pages._note: DCAT-AP publication record — the app's core artifact. Bespoke publication/document archetype: the body leads with the catalog metadata (title, summary, description, organization, themes, publish/depublish dates, retention) as the 8-wide primary block, with a compact Related panel (resolves the organization publisher + linked themes) alongside it. Below sits the full-width Attachments block — a publication IS its distributables, so Files (documents/downloads) is the dominant secondary surface, followed by Images (photos) and external Links (bookmarks) side-by-side for the DCAT distribution set. No email/calendar/talk/contacts: the publication schema declares NO linkedTypes, and a publication is metadata about a document, not a correspondence thread (HARD RULE 1). geo/maps dropped — the schema has no geo field. Sidebar keeps read-only metadata, Activity, Shares and an Audit-trail leaf (publications are FOI/WOO records where change history matters — kept in the sidebar per HARD RULE 4). Note: photos/bookmarks/files are OpenRegister integration leaves, so they render via widgetKey:"integration" with props.only — the previous bare widgetKeys (maps/photos/bookmarks/file-manager) were unresolved no-ops.

pages.title: Search
pages._note: FederationSearch (custom) wraps CnSearchPage with the federation-aware search store so /search hits /api/federation/publications. The previous type:'search' default fanned out per-schema against the local OpenRegister, bypassing federation entirely.

pages.title: Organization
pages._note: Publisher-organization archetype (the legal entity behind catalogs/publications: name, summary, description, and the government identifiers OIN/TOOI/RSIN/PKI). The body leads with the identity block (8-wide, 2-column so the four registration codes read cleanly) and a Related panel (4-wide) that surfaces the publications and catalogs pointing at this organization. Below, the full-width Images leaf carries the organization logo/branding (the schema's `image` field is a single logo, so Images is the right surface, not Files). NO contacts widget: the organization schema declares NO linkedTypes and has no contactpersoon relation, so a Contacts leaf would render empty (the previous widgetKey:"contacts" was an unresolved no-op anyway) — per HARD RULE 1 comms/contacts only when the schema declares them. Sidebar keeps read-only metadata plus an Activity tab (org records change rarely but syncs matter for federation).

pages.title: Theme
pages._note: Catalog-theme / presentation archetype — a visual grouping used to categorise and style publications (title, summary, description, image, content, link/url, icon). This is a configuration+content record, so the body pairs the editable fields (6-wide) with the live theme-preview custom widget (6-wide) so an editor sees the rendered card/banner as they tune it — the reason theme-preview exists. Below, a full-width Related panel surfaces the publications tagged with this theme. Deliberately NO comms and NO Files/audit: a theme communicates with nobody, declares no linkedTypes, and its `image`/`content` live in the record itself — keeping it clean per HARD RULE 1/2. Sidebar keeps read-only metadata only.

pages.title: Glossary term
pages._note: Glossary-term / reference archetype — a single defined concept (title, summary, description, externalLink, keywords) used to explain vocabulary across the catalog. A term's value is its definition plus how it connects to other terms, so the body pairs the definition fields (6-wide) with the relationship-graph custom widget (6-wide) that visualises related terms — the reason that widget exists. Below, a full-width Related panel lists publications/themes that reference this term. Intentionally the leanest detail page: NO comms, NO Files, NO extra tabs — a glossary entry declares no linkedTypes, has no attachments, and communicates with nobody (HARD RULES 1 & 2). Sidebar keeps read-only metadata only.

pages.title: Page
pages._note: CMS-page / structured-content archetype — a portal page (title, slug, visibility flags hideBeforeLogin/hideAfterLogin, access groups) whose body is an ordered list of embedded content blocks (`contents[]` — inline objects, NOT a separate register schema, so an object-list child table cannot be used). Previously this page had NO widgets and fell back to the generic data dump. Now the body pairs the page settings (5-wide, 1-column — slug + visibility read as a config sheet) with the tree-view custom widget (7-wide) that renders the nested content-block structure so an editor can see and reorder the page composition. NO comms/Files/related: a portal page declares no linkedTypes, its content is inline, and it communicates with nobody (HARD RULE 1). Sidebar keeps read-only metadata only.

pages.title: Menu
pages._note: Navigation-menu / structure archetype — a portal menu (title, position, visibility flag hideBeforeLogin, access groups) whose substance is its ordered, nestable `items[]` (inline objects with order/name/link, NOT a separate register schema — so no object-list child table). The body pairs the menu settings (4-wide, 1-column — position/visibility read as a config sheet) with a wide tree-view (8-wide) that renders and lets an editor reorder the item hierarchy, since the tree IS the menu. NO comms/Files/related: a menu declares no linkedTypes, its items are inline, and it communicates with nobody (HARD RULE 1). Sidebar keeps read-only metadata only. Widened the tree vs the settings block (was 6/6) because the item hierarchy is the primary content here.

pages.title: Directory
pages._note: FederationDirectory (custom) fetches /api/listings and renders CnFederationStatus with mapped nodes + an admin add-directory modal. The bare CnFederationStatus this replaced shipped a permanent empty-state because the manifest had no :nodes binding.
